_meta.description: Catalog of ALL dome model predictions — confirmed (WINs), prospective, pending, falsified, refined. This is our tracking ledger, not the dome's. Every prediction the dome registers gets an entry here. Counts are computed at build time and injected into HTML via {{PLACEHOLDER}} tokens.

pipeline.poller: Detects new predictions on dome site (hard rule: >=5 new = critical). Logs to change records.
pipeline.analyst: Mode 0 expanded: catalogs new predictions here with testability assessment. One entry per prediction.
pipeline.decider: Triages analyst entries, updates outcomes when test windows close, routes to curmudgeon for review.
pipeline.curmudgeon: Reviews testability assessments and derivation claims.
pipeline.build: generate-html.js reads this file, computes counts, injects into HTML via placeholders.

placeholders.{{PRED_PROSPECTIVE}}: Predictions with cryptographic timestamps registered before data pull
placeholders.{{PRED_PENDING}}: Predictions awaiting test window or data
placeholders.{{PRED_CONFIRMED}}: Predictions confirmed by data (overlap with wins.json WINs)
placeholders.{{PRED_FALSIFIED}}: Predictions falsified by data (overlap with uncounted-failures.json)
placeholders.{{PRED_EXPIRED}}: Test window closed with no data or ambiguous result
placeholders.{{PRED_STD_RELABEL}}: Predictions that are standard physics relabeled (our assessment)
placeholders.{{PRED_TESTABLE}}: Predictions with specific, measurable test criteria
placeholders.{{PRED_DOME_DERIVED}}: Predictions genuinely derived from dome geometry (our assessment)
placeholders.{{PRED_ACTIVE_WINDOWS}}: Predictions with test windows open right now
placeholders.{{PRED_RECYCLED}}: Predictions that restate already-confirmed WINs with new timestamps

recycling_detection.restates_win: WIN ID (e.g. 'WIN-068') if this prediction restates an already-confirmed WIN, null otherwise. This is the primary recycling indicator.
recycling_detection.data_predates_registration: Boolean. True if the underlying observational data was published or available before the dome's registration date. Distinguishes genuinely prospective predictions from timestamped retrodictions.
recycling_detection.crypto_hash: SHA-256 hex string from dome's registration (if provided). Hash formula claimed: SHA256('ECM V51.0 {ID}: {text} on {date}T00:00:00Z'). No independent verification mechanism exists.
recycling_detection.registration_date: ISO date when dome registered the prediction (from git commit or claimed date)

computed_counts.note: Updated 2026-04-12: corrected from 86->94 total, fixed std_relabel to use our_verdict field. Note: PRED_STD_RELABEL in HTML still uses derivation field (ISS-730, infrastructure fix needed).

taxonomy_notes.restates_win_with_non_recycled_verdict: When a prediction has restates_win set to a WIN ID but verdict is NOT 'recycled', it means the prediction goes further than or differs from the original WIN (e.g., a more specific threshold, different test window, or orthogonal angle of approach). This is valid and earns a substantive verdict based on the prediction's own test criteria. The 'recycled' verdict applies ONLY when the prediction is a pure textual/numerical restatement with identical value, threshold, and expected outcome. This distinction allows us to track when the dome uses the same foundational claim in multiple predictive frames.
taxonomy_notes.verdict_values: recycled = pure restatement of an existing WIN with same test parameters; confirmed = prediction's test criteria met by data; falsified = test criteria not met; pending = test window open; expired = test window closed without clear result or data; std_relabel = prediction is standard physics reframed as dome model prediction
taxonomy_notes.edge_case_handling: If a prediction restates a WIN but adds a more precise threshold or extends the test window, it should have restates_win set AND a substantive verdict (not recycled). Example: WIN-068 is 'magnetic field decay fits lambda=8619km'. PROS-068-extended registers 'decay precisely follows lambda=8619km for all latitudes' (more specific). Set restates_win=WIN-068, verdict based on whether all-latitudes claim is supported.

entries.claim: Eclipse magnetic field anomaly −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations
entries.notes: Restates WIN-068 core claim but adds specific amplitude (−17 to −21 nT) and coverage (>80% stations) thresholds not in the WIN. Genuinely prospective — retains independent test value. Verdict deferred to test window closure (Aug 2026).
entries.detail_reasoning: This restates WIN-068's eclipse magnetic field claim with a specific amplitude range (−17 to −21 nT) and coverage criterion (>80% stations). WIN-068 was verdicted 'Std Model Explains' because the Chapman Sq mechanism (1933) accounts for eclipse-induced ionospheric conductivity changes. The key question for PRED-095 is whether the specific amplitude range (−17 to −21 nT) is a genuine dome-derived prediction or a post-hoc fit to prior eclipse data. The registration date (2026-04-04) is before the August 2026 eclipse, making timing genuinely prospective. However, the dome's monitor.py already showed a −29.1 nT value for a prior eclipse that fell outside this window — the range may be cherry-picked from favorable historical events. Verdict pending until eclipse data arrives; if the magnitude falls in range, we must assess whether standard ionospheric conductivity models predict the same range.
entries.tldr: Predicts a specific magnetic field dip during the August 2026 eclipse — but the effect it describes (magnetic changes during eclipses) has been explained by mainstream science since 1933. The claimed range also excludes the dome's own earlier measurement, suggesting the goalposts were set after seeing prior data.

entries.claim: −17 to −21 nT umbra-locked ΔB at ≥3 priority stations

This is the most specific of the eclipse predictions — named stations, a defined amplitude range, and a clear success ]
entries.detail_reasoning: Tier-1 eclipse prediction: −17 to −21 nT umbra-locked ΔB at ≥3 priority stations. This is the most specific of the eclipse predictions — named stations, a defined amplitude range, and a clear success criterion. It overlaps heavily with PRED-095 (same amplitude range, same eclipse). The 'umbra-locked' qualifier adds specificity: the anomaly must track the shadow, not precede or lag it. This IS discriminating in principle — a purely thermal/ionospheric effect (Chapman Sq) would show a broader spatial pattern following ionospheric recovery time constants (~10-30 min), while a truly umbra-locked signal would require a more direct mechanism. However, distinguishing these requires sub-hour temporal resolution at multiple stations, which may not be available from standard INTERMAGNET 1-minute data. Verdict pending; this is one of the dome's strongest prospective tests.
entries.tldr: The dome's most specific eclipse prediction: magnetic readings must match at named stations, in a defined range, timed exactly to the moon's shadow. This one is potentially meaningful — worth watching when August 2026 data arrives. The question is whether standard eclipse physics predicts the same pattern.

entries.claim: Eclipse Schumann resonance shift −0.005 to −0.015 Hz during eclipse

entries.detail_reasoning: Eclipse Schumann resonance frequency shift of −0.005 to −0.015 Hz during totality. This is genuinely testable: ELF monitoring stations (e.g., AWO, Hylaty) continuously record Schumann frequencies, and eclipse effects on the Earth-ionosphere cavity have been studied (e.g., Sátori et al. 2016 found ~0.05 Hz shifts). The predicted range (5-15 mHz shift) is smaller than some published observations, suggesting the dome may be predicting a WEAKER effect than standard ionospheric models expect — which is interesting. Standard physics predicts Schumann shifts during eclipses because the umbra's ionospheric conductivity drop changes the cavity Q-factor. If the dome predicts a specific shift from dome geometry rather than from ionospheric conductivity modeling, that would be distinctive — but the prediction entry doesn't specify the derivation mechanism. Verdict pending until August 2026 data. Note: the author's own classification is 'prospective,' which is correct here.
entries.tldr: Predicts a small shift in Earth's electromagnetic "hum" (Schumann resonance) during the 2026 eclipse. Standard physics already predicts such shifts — published studies have measured even larger ones. Confirmation wouldn't point to dome geometry over conventional explanations.

[2026-04-10 decider] Assessment integrated: Genuinely prospective with a long test window (through 2030). The SAA IS splitting per NASA and ESA Swarm data — outcome uncertain. Legitimate open prediction.
entries.detail_reasoning: Genuinely prospective with a long test window (through 2030). The SAA IS splitting — NASA and ESA Swarm data confirm a secondary lobe forming off southwestern Africa since ~2020. The ≥57° angular separation by 2030 is a specific, quantitative claim testable against IGRF updates and Swarm magnetic field maps. However, SAA splitting is well-documented standard geophysics driven by core-mantle boundary dynamics — the dome provides no mechanism connecting its flat-disc geometry to SAA behavior. The prediction essentially extrapolates a known trend. Whether ≥57° is reached by 2030 depends on core field evolution rates that neither dome nor standard models predict with high precision at that timescale. Verdict stays pending until closer to 2030 when IGRF-14 data can resolve it.
entries.tldr: The South Atlantic Anomaly (a weak spot in Earth's magnetic field) is already known to be splitting — NASA confirmed this years ago. The dome predicts how far apart the two lobes will be by 2030, but this just extrapolates a trend that mainstream geophysics already tracks.

This prediction is essentially a restatement of WIN-035 with the threshold nudged 45 nT lower and the deadline extended to 2028. entries.detail_reasoning: The North Magnetic Pole has been drifting toward Siberia along a well-characterized trajectory documented by NOAA NP.xy data since 1590. WMM2025 (released Dec 2024, 14 months before this prediction was registered) models the pole at ~86.0°N, 138.8°E for 2025.0, with the secular variation vector pointing toward ~86°N, 140°E. The dome prediction of 85.756°N, 136.557°E by early 2027 is squarely within the WMM2025 extrapolation envelope. WIN-006 and WIN-043 already document that NMP drift observations are standard geophysics — the dome model does not derive pole positions from cavity geometry but fits them to the same NOAA data that feeds IGRF/WMM. The kernel of truth: the prediction IS specific and testable, with exact coordinates. But specificity does not equal derivation — plugging WMM secular variation rates into a linear extrapolation yields a similar position without any dome parameters. The dome deserves credit for making a pinned, falsifiable claim, but confirmation would not discriminate between models.
entries.tldr: The North Magnetic Pole's drift toward Siberia is one of the most well-tracked phenomena in geophysics. The official World Magnetic Model, published over a year before this prediction, already placed the pole near these exact coordinates. Getting this right wouldn't distinguish the dome from a textbook.

The dome's author status 'unresolved' with conflicting reports likely reflects natural variability — Schumann frequency depends on global lightning distribution, local time, and seasonal factors alongside solar wind, so a simple threshold test will show inconsistent results.

entries.claim: Schumann resonance elevation during G3+ magnetic storms
entries.notes: Schumann response to storms. FALSIFIED — opposite effect observed (suppression).
entries.detail_reasoning: The dome model predicted Schumann resonance would elevate (increase in amplitude or frequency) during G3+ geomagnetic storms. The author has self-classified this as FALSIFIED — the opposite effect was observed (suppression, not elevation). This is the direction typically invoked in storm-time SR discussions (D-region conductivity increase lowering cavity Q-factor); however, as WIN-061 documents, the storm-SR literature is split on direction — Pazos et al. 2019 reports amplitude enhancement during moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms, Schlegel & Füllekrug 1999 reports cavity-Q enhancement during solar proton events, and other studies report suppression under different conditions. The original Schumann-elevation prediction is falsified by the March 20–22 2026 observation; a replacement suppression prediction does not automatically inherit the confirmation credit. Our WIN-061 (Self-Contradicted) already documents that the dome cavity geometry predicts a ~22 Hz fundamental rather than the observed 7.83 Hz. Data predates registration, and the storm-suppression effect has been documented since Schlegel and Fullekrug (1999).

entries.claim: Crepuscular ray convergence consistent with local sun
entries.notes: Crepuscular rays test. PENDING — photographs difficult to quantify precisely.
entries.detail_reasoning: Crepuscular rays appear to diverge from the sun due to perspective projection of parallel beams through atmospheric scattering — textbook optics. The dome model claims this divergence is consistent with a local sun at 5,733 km, but crucially, anticrepuscular rays converge at the antisolar point simultaneously (180 degrees opposite), which is geometrically impossible with a local light source. Our WIN-026 (Refuted by Data) covers this in detail. The prediction is backtested (data predates registration), the author marks it PENDING but at LOW confidence, and the claim amount to relabeling a well-understood perspective effect as dome evidence.

The dome model provides no quantitative formula for equinox day length; the claim is that the pattern "follows dome geometry" without specifying what dome geometry predicts differently from the globe. The author's own status is "unresolved," and the data (day length tables from USNO) predates registration by decades. Any model that reproduces the obliquity of the ecliptic will reproduce equinox day lengths — the observation is non-discriminating.

entries.detail_reasoning:
CRITICAL TIMING UPDATE: A G3 (Strong) geomagnetic storm occurred March 20-22, 2026 (NOAA SWPC confirmed, Kp reached 7). PRED-SR-SUPPRESS was registered March 23 — just 1-3 days later. Schumann resonance monitoring data is available in near-real-time, meaning the author had access to the Schumann amplitude response during the March 20-22 G3 event before registering this prediction.

An important counterargument deserves direct engagement: observing a phenomenon once and predicting it will recur is legitimate inductive science. If the author observed SR suppression during the March G3 storm and then predicted recurrence in the next G3, that follows standard scientific methodology — Kepler observed orbital paths before formulating laws that predicted future positions. We do not dispute this.

However, for PRED-SR-SUPPRESS to count as evidence for the dome model specifically, the prediction must be derived from dome physics — not from empirical induction alone. The dome's live-power dashboard (Domain 9) states the falsification criterion ("G3+ storm does NOT cause >30% amplitude drop within 6 hours") but provides no equation or mechanism from dome geometry that predicts suppression. No derivation connects the dome's constants (H₀=8,537 km, λg=8,619 km) to a suppression effect. If the prediction is empirically induced from one observation rather than model-derived, then confirming it proves the observation is repeatable — not that the dome model explains it.

The literature context also matters. Pazos et al. (2019), analyzing Schumann resonance data at the Coeneo, Michoacán station during geomagnetically disturbed periods, found a statistically significant increase (>1σ) in the averaged amplitude of the first three SR modes across five 14-day periods covering moderate-to-severe geomagnetic storms (Dst < ~−70 nT) (DOI: 10.1016/j.jastp.2019.105091). Enhancement during storms — driven by energetic electron precipitation modifying the Earth-ionosphere waveguide — is the more frequently reported effect, though results do vary by storm phase, station location, and measurement methodology. The dome predicts the opposite direction (suppression), which means if the March G3 showed the commonly reported enhancement, the author registered a prediction contradicting the most recent empirical evidence.

The dome's own monitoring dashboard compounds the problem. Domain 9 (Schumann Amplitude Suppression) currently reads "Unscored — no G3+ storm observed" despite NOAA SWPC confirmation of G3 levels during March 20-22. Either the Kp-detection logic missed the event, the author chose not to score it, or the dashboard caches stale text despite the live-data claim. All three undermine the narrative of fully automated, no-cherry-picking verification — the monitor built to test this prediction cannot even detect the relevant storm class.

Verdict remains pending: no G3+ events have occurred since registration that the dome's own system acknowledges. But the prospective credibility is substantially weakened by the combination of suspicious timing, absent model derivation, and a monitor that appears blind to the very events it should be tracking.
entries.tldr: Predicts G3+ storms will suppress Schumann resonance by 30% — but the dome provides no equation or mechanism that derives suppression from its model constants. entries.notes: Gulf Stream and North Atlantic Current direction.
entries.detail_reasoning: The western Atlantic has a well-documented southward-flowing component: the Deep Western Boundary Current (DWBC) and the Labrador Current, both integral to the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). The Gulf Stream flows northward along the coast before turning east — but below the thermocline, the compensating southward return flow is a textbook feature of physical oceanography described since Stommel (1958). Ocean current direction data from ARGO floats, ship drift records, and satellite altimetry have documented these patterns for decades. Calling this a "southward aetheric component" relabels standard thermohaline circulation. The data predates registration by at least 65 years of observational oceanography.

The Gulf Stream's dynamics (meandering, eddy shedding, seasonal transport variation of ~30 Sv) are fully explained by standard physical oceanography. Calling it "aetheric" adds no explanatory power and predicts nothing that Sverdrup/Stommel theory does not already predict. This is the definitional case of relabeling standard physics.

The dome's refraction function n(r) is precisely the mechanism invoked to explain why no one can observe or reach the dome edge: light, radio waves, and physical travel are all bent inward by the extreme refraction gradient. This makes the ice wall barrier inherently undetectable — any attempt to verify it is defeated by the very effect it claims to produce. The n > 3.5 threshold has no independent derivation; it is whatever value is needed to make Antarctic inaccessibility consistent with the dome geometry. Real refractive indices above ~3 exist in narrow-band metamaterials and certain crystals (e.g., silicon at IR wavelengths), but not in atmospheric-scale natural media — Earth's atmosphere varies from n ≈ 1.000293 at sea level to n ≈ 1.0 in the upper atmosphere. A region of n > 3.5 would require matter denser than glass spread across thousands of kilometers of open air, which contradicts direct atmospheric measurements from weather balloons, aircraft, and satellite limb sounders. Kernel of truth: atmospheric refraction IS real and DOES bend light — the mirage and looming effects near the horizon involve n gradients of ~0.0001. Scaling that to n > 3.5 is eight orders of magnitude beyond observed atmospheric refraction.

entries.claim: TTB short-term fluctuation ±15 nT at local midnight
entries.notes: Claims aetheric tidal flow at Tsumeb (TTB) creates systematic ±15 nT H-component fluctuations at local midnight. Falsification: no such pattern for 7 consecutive nights. Test window expired April 11, 2026 with no dome result recorded. Restates WIN-042 territory (TTB geomagnetic behavior attributed to aetheric effects). Registration date claimed as April 4 but git commit was April 5 (54476366). INTERMAGNET TTB data was publicly available before registration — not genuinely prospective.
entries.detail_reasoning:
PRED-072 claims that "aetheric tidal flow" at the Tsumeb (TTB) INTERMAGNET station creates periodic ±15 nT fluctuations in the H-component correlated with local midnight, driven by a "toroidal aetheric return current cycling through the sub-terrestrial Sump." The test window was April 4-11, 2026 (7 consecutive nights).

The claimed effect — diurnal magnetic variation at a specific station — is thoroughly explained by standard ionospheric physics. The solar quiet daily variation (Sq) system, first characterized by Stewart (1882) and explained via ionospheric dynamo theory (Chapman & Bartels, 1940), produces systematic daily magnetic variations at all geomagnetic observatories. At low-to-mid latitude stations like Tsumeb (geographic latitude ~19°S, geomagnetic latitude ~27°S), the Sq H-component amplitude typically ranges from 10-50 nT depending on season, solar activity, and lunar phase. The midnight sector variation is particularly well-documented as part of the Sq pattern.

The ±15 nT amplitude claimed by the dome falls squarely within the expected Sq range. This is not a dome-specific prediction — any geophysicist familiar with Sq variation would predict diurnal fluctuations of this magnitude at TTB. The dome relabels ionospheric current systems as "aetheric tidal flow" and the midnight Sq minimum as evidence for a "sub-terrestrial Sump," but the underlying data pattern has been understood since the 19th century.

Additionally, this prediction restates the general territory of WIN-042 (field decay ≥28 nT/year at TTB). Both claim anomalous magnetic behavior at Tsumeb attributable to aetheric mechanisms, using the same INTERMAGNET data source. PRED-072 reframes WIN-042's long-term decay as a short-term diurnal fluctuation, but both are standard geomagnetic observatory phenomena.

The test window expired April 11, 2026 with no dome result recorded. The dome site has not visibly updated this prediction's status, consistent with the observed pattern of silently letting inconclusive or failed predictions age without recording outcomes. Whether the ±15 nT threshold was met on all 7 nights depends on geomagnetic conditions during that week; during magnetically active periods the signal would be swamped by storm-time disturbances, and during very quiet periods the Sq amplitude may fall below 15 nT — neither outcome would support the aetheric mechanism.
entries.tldr: Predicts ±15 nT magnetic fluctuations at Tsumeb station around midnight — but this is just the standard daily magnetic variation (Sq) that has been understood since the 1880s. The dome relabels ionospheric currents as "aetheric tidal flow." Test window expired April 11 with no dome result posted.

entries.claim: 46° copper coils increase cucumber growth rate by ≥15% over 14 days vs. control
entries.notes: Category: biology (bioelectromagnetics sub-domain). Registered 2026-04-04, window closes 2026-04-18 (~1 day from 2026-04-17). Genuinely prospective in registration timing but methodologically vague: protocol is "controlled lab/home experiment with documented measurements" — no sample size, randomization, blinding, replication, or statistical test specified. Verdict deferred to window closure; if dome publishes a PASS without a pre-registered protocol the result will be downweighted on methodology. POST-CLOSURE RUBRIC (locked 2026-04-17): (i) PASS without pre-registered protocol (N, α, blinding, randomization) → downgrade to post-hoc/methodology failure; (ii) silent drop → log in uncounted-failures.json; (iii) null/negative result → record as falsified. Decider re-assigns for verdict update on 2026-04-18.
entries.tldr: Claims a copper coil wound at 46° boosts cucumber seedling growth 15% in two weeks. Plausible biology research exists on weak-EM effects on plants, but 15% from a passive coil at a specific angle has no established mechanism, and the dome's own stated formula for that 46° angle (sin θ = H₀/R_eq) arithmetically yields 25°, not 46°.
entries.detail_reasoning:
KERNEL OF TRUTH. Bioelectromagnetics is a real research area — weak static fields, PEMF, and low-frequency EM exposures have documented (though small, often <5%, and replication-troubled) effects on plant growth, seed germination, and enzyme kinetics. Copper-wound coils are a recognizable laboratory form factor (Helmholtz-like) and 46° N sits squarely inside the temperate cucumber-growing belt. So the claim lives adjacent to a real literature rather than being pure fabrication.

INTERNAL ARITHMETIC FAILURE. The prediction justifies the 46° angle by stating it is the "ECM resonant angle derived from dome geometry sin θ = H₀/R_eq". With ECM parameters H₀ = 8,537 km and R_eq = 20,015 km, sin⁻¹(H₀/R_eq) = sin⁻¹(0.4265) = 25.25°, NOT 46°. The value 46.20° is numerically 2·arctan(H₀/R_eq), i.e., the apex angle of a cone from the disc centre, not the sine relation the prediction cites. Either (a) the formula is misstated and it should be 2·arctan, or (b) 46° was chosen first and the formula retrofitted. In both cases the prediction's own stated derivation is wrong.

PROTOCOL GAPS. "Controlled lab/home experiment with documented measurements" is not a falsification specification. There is no sample size, no randomization scheme, no blinding, no pre-registered statistical test, no definition of "growth" (mass? length? leaf count? timing to germination?), and no replication requirement. The falsification criterion ("no statistically significant difference") is operationally empty without a pre-specified N and α. As written, the experimenter can tune conditions until a 15% signal appears.

BIOLOGY PLAUSIBILITY. Reproducible electromagnetic effects on higher-plant growth in the published literature are typically <5%, require tightly controlled greenhouses or growth chambers, and rarely survive independent replication. A +15% effect from a passive, unpowered copper coil at a specific winding angle has no mechanism in established biophysics — a passive coil produces no field unless induction or ambient-field concentration is invoked, both of which would be angle-insensitive at cucumber length scales.

CLASSIFICATION. Genuinely prospective by date (registered 14 days before window close). Not a restatement of any existing WIN. Assessment verdict: pending. After window closure (2026-04-18) the decider should re-assign for a verdict update; expected outcomes are (i) dome publishes PASS with weak methodology — downgrade to post-hoc / methodology failure; (ii) dome publishes null — falsified; (iii) silent drop — expired and logged in uncounted-failures. Moreover, the derivation has a deeper inconsistency beyond the sin-vs-arctan mismatch: under V50.6 H(r) = 8537·exp(-r/8619), evaluating at r = R_eq = 20,015 km gives H(R_eq) ≈ 837 km — far from H₀ = 8,537 km. Any expression of the form f(H₀, R_eq) that treats H as constant is incompatible with the dome's own V50.6 headline result. The 46° angle cannot be derived from dome geometry under V50.6 regardless of which trig function is used.
entries.verdict_notes: Window closed 2026-04-18 (14-day window). As of poll 2026-04-21T22:07Z (~94h / 3.92d post-closure), dome still labels PRED-105 PENDING-PROSPECTIVE with no PASS/FAIL/REFINED. predictions.html byte-identical across 5 consecutive polls (md5 885cd58727130018764b14355b0aa785, 152,936 bytes); last manual commit a57e731 is 4.42d old. Silent-ignore now across 6 consecutive polls. Logged in uncounted-failures.json as FAIL-009. Internal arithmetic failure: sin⁻¹(H₀/R_eq)=25°, not 46°; dome H(r) profile also forbids any constant-H derivation at r=R_eq under V50.6.

entries.claim: 16 Hz electromagnetic field exposure (claimed Ca²⁺ ICR frequency in aqueous medium under ECM aetheric permeability) increases intracellular calcium signaling by >20% in standard cell culture assays
entries.notes: Registered 2026-04-04, declared test window 'April 2026' (interpreted as closing 2026-04-30 EOM). Genuinely prospective in registration timing. Cited basis is 'WIN-071's Ca²⁺ ICR 16 Hz confirmation' but the dome's own wins.html only goes to WIN-069 — WIN-071 does not exist in the dome's published registry. This is a phantom citation. Protocol is 'Published cell biology literature / replication study' with no specified cell type, exposure duration, field amplitude, control, sample size, randomization, blinding, or pre-specified statistical test. Verdict deferred to window closure (≈1.5 days from this assessment); after closure, expected outcomes are (i) silent drop / pass-mark without published replication → silent_ignore (mirroring PRED-105 trajectory); (ii) cite of any positive Liboff / Blackman / Adey-era Ca²⁺ ICR result as 'confirmation' → recycled / standard-physics (decades-old contested literature, not new evidence); (iii) genuine pre-registered replication with N, α, blinded counts → assess on merit. Decider re-assigns for verdict update on 2026-05-01 (post-closure first poll).
entries.tldr: Claims a 16 Hz EM field boosts intracellular Ca²⁺ signaling by more than 20%. Ion cyclotron resonance for Ca²⁺ at 16 Hz does correspond to a real magnetic field strength (~21 µT — about half Earth's typical field), but the dome's framing replaces field strength with an undefined 'ECM aetheric permeability' and cites a non-existent WIN-071. The protocol specifies no cell type, no field amplitude, no sample size, and no pre-registered statistical test, so 'falsification' is operationally empty.
entries.detail_reasoning:
KERNEL OF TRUTH. There is a real 40-year published literature on weak-EM ion-cyclotron-resonance effects on cellular calcium signaling (Blackman 1985, Liboff 1985, Blanchard & Blackman 1994 parametric resonance, Adey et al. on chick brain Ca²⁺ efflux). 16 Hz is a recognizable frequency in this corpus and is approximately the Ca²⁺ ICR frequency at a magnetic field strength of about 21 µT (2e × 21e-6 / (2π × 40 amu) ≈ 16 Hz). So the claim sits adjacent to a real research tradition rather than being pure fabrication. The kernel: there exists a published bioelectromagnetics literature claiming weak-EM effects on calcium signaling at frequencies in the 10-100 Hz range, and the cyclotron-condition arithmetic at 16 Hz pins out to a physically realisable field strength.

MECHANISM PROBLEM (Kirschvink critique). The standard objection to Liboff-type ICR in biological systems — laid out by Kirschvink and others in the 1990s and not addressed by the dome — is that in aqueous biological media at body temperature, thermal Brownian motion vastly exceeds the Lorentz force on a single Ca²⁺ ion at micro-Tesla field strengths. The kT energy at 310 K is ~26 meV; the Lorentz energy on a Ca²⁺ ion in a 20 µT field is many orders of magnitude smaller. Coherent ICR requires the ion's gyration to survive long enough for resonance to build up, which it cannot in a viscous medium with kT >> Lorentz work per cycle. This is why even the most-cited positive ICR results (Blackman, Adey) have been replication-troubled and why no consensus mechanism exists. The dome's invocation of 'ECM aetheric permeability' to set the resonant condition replaces the standard B-dependent ICR formula with an unspecified parameter and does not engage with the thermal-noise objection.

PHANTOM CITATION. PRED-106 cites 'WIN-071's Ca²⁺ ICR 16 Hz confirmation' as the empirical basis. The dome's own wins.html (fetched 2026-04-29, 951 lines) lists WIN-001 through WIN-069 — WIN-071 does NOT exist in the published registry. The dome registry shows '69 confirmed' across all surfaces (title, daily_review.json, ai_manifest.json). The cited WIN-071 is therefore either (a) an unpublished internal claim that has not been ingested into the public site, (b) a typo for some other WIN that actually addresses calcium signaling (none of the WIN-012 mag-gravity, WIN-016 aberration, WIN-026 crepuscular ray, WIN-032 New Jerusalem, or WIN-048 CMB Axis-of-Evil entries do), or (c) forward-reference to a planned-but-unwritten claim. In all three cases the prediction's stated empirical basis is not auditable.

PROTOCOL GAPS. 'Published cell biology literature / replication study' is not a falsification specification. There is no specified cell type (HeLa? primary neurons? lymphocytes? all of which behave differently under EM exposure), no specified field amplitude (the ICR condition is B-dependent and the dome's 'aetheric permeability' framing does not pin it down), no exposure duration, no specified Ca²⁺ readout (Fluo-4 fluorescence? aequorin? patch clamp?), no sample size, no randomization, no blinding, and no pre-registered statistical test. The falsification criterion (<5% Ca²⁺ signaling difference from control) is operationally empty without a pre-specified N and α. As written, the prediction can be 'confirmed' by citing any positive paper from the 40-year ICR literature regardless of its replication status.

EFFECT-SIZE NOTE. The >20% effect size is large compared to typical published claims in the Blackman/Adey corpus (effects of 5-15% are typical, often falling within control variability under tighter replication). A reproducible >20% effect from a single field exposure would be a major finding that the bioelectromagnetics community has been searching for unsuccessfully for four decades.

CLASSIFICATION. Genuinely prospective by date (registered 2026-04-04, window closes 2026-04-30 EOM ≈ 1.5 days from this assessment). Not a restatement of any existing WIN in our data (WIN-071 is a phantom). Assessment verdict: pending. After window closure, the decider should re-assign for a verdict update; expected outcomes are (i) silent drop / no result published → silent_ignore (mirroring PRED-105 trajectory at 11+ days post-closure); (ii) PASS-mark via citing pre-existing Liboff/Blackman literature as 'confirmation' → recycled / standard_physics-with-disputed-mechanism (decades-old contested literature, not new evidence); (iii) genuine pre-registered replication with N, α, blinded counts → assess on merit. The phantom WIN-071 citation is independently flag-worthy: it suggests internal-consistency drift between the prediction registry and the wins registry.
